name: Pade approximation arrow termination theorem
subsection_title: Order arrows and the Ehle barrier
statement_text:
Let R(z) denote a Padé approximation to exp(z), with
degrees n (numerator) and d (denominator). Then n of the down arrows
terminate at zeros and d of the up arrows terminate at poles.
proof_text:
Proof. Because p = n + d, n ≥           it follows from Theorem 355D
                              n and d ≥ d,
that
                         p=n+d≥    n + d ≥ p
                    ) + (d − 
and hence that (n − n         d) = 0. Since both terms are non-negative they
must be zero and the result follows.
proof_latex:
\begin{proof}
Because \( p = n + d \), \( n \geq \widehat{n} \) and \( d \geq \widehat{d} \), it follows from Theorem~\ref{thm:355D} that
\[
p = n + d \geq \widehat{n} + \widehat{d} \geq p
\]
and hence that \( (n - \widehat{n}) + (d - \widehat{d}) = 0 \). Since both terms are non-negative they must be zero and the result follows.

Before proving the `Ehle barrier', we establish a criterion for A-stability based on the up arrows that terminate at poles.
\end{proof}
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
Order star analysis of Pad\'e approximations $R(z)$ to $\exp(z)$, with $\deg(\text{numerator})=n$, $\deg(\text{denominator})=d$, and $p=n+d$. Down arrows: $|R(z)|>|\exp(z)|$; up arrows: $|R(z)|<|\exp(z)|$. Let $\tilde{n}$ be the number of down arrows terminating at zeros of $R$, and $\tilde{d}$ the number of up arrows terminating at poles of $R$.

variables.name: R(z)
variables.definition: a Padé approximation to exp(z), with degrees n (numerator) and d (denominator)

variables.name: down arrows
variables.definition: arrows in the order star of R, representing directions where |R(z)| > |exp(z)|

variables.name: up arrows
variables.definition: arrows in the order star of R, representing directions where |R(z)| < |exp(z)|

variables.name: ñ
variables.definition: number of down arrows terminating at zeros of R

variables.name: d̃
variables.definition: number of up arrows terminating at poles of R

preamble: The context involves the order star analysis of Padé approximations to exp(z), where arrows (down and up) represent regions where the approximation's magnitude is greater or less than exp(z). The theorem relates the counts of arrows terminating at zeros and poles to the degrees of the numerator and denominator.

dependencies.name: Down arrow zero up arrow pole inequality
